speaker: interviewer
text: --ka, tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man that I married-- which was not a real marriage, I hope that's clear-- was Wilhelm Kuhn, K-U-H-N. I thought he was very decent in doing that. And I let him know that, but I said, 'Don't ever touch me.' And that just-- I just couldn't take that. And he understood that. We didn't have-- he could go to Vienna first. We didn't have to live together because this other friend, who also went through the same thing as I, said we do have to live together for a while. I don't know how we explained it. Her husband lived in Paris. And we said my husband hasn't yet found an apartment or something. I don't know anymore. But we lived in another-- so we never lived together. I did see him a few times, yes, in Vienna, and then later, you know, in Paris, much later. I felt that I wanted to have it clear that we were divorced. And we got a divorce on grounds of that he is not Jewish and I am Jewish. So the Nazis would give that very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the year, but it is before I entered the United States, which was very good because I didn't have to go under Kuhn to the United States. I got under my maiden name to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you married him, you were able to go into Austria, which was your intention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in Vienna-- and I want to make that faster because I'm afraid I will never get through with this. When I was very poor, people were singing wonderful songs, but in the street corners to get a few pennies. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, as usual, could do something by-- give me the word again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tutoring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tutoring. What's the matter with me? That word gets away. All right, by tutoring. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These were Jewish families, and they already felt fear. But it wasn't yet there, but I-- we saw that it was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna. I don't know the street anymore. It was in an apartment of a very nice landlady. And, actually, it was a nice room, a beautiful room. We shared that, Erna and I. And I gave those. I was in two places where I did the tutoring. Got some money. I-- I can't tell the details anymore because it's a comparatively short time because we saw that-- we knew that the Nazis were coming. You know, again, I am not that nice about Schuschnigg as some of the history books are because he wanted to play it, I think. At least, that's the way we felt with Hitler, you know, just hoping that nothing would happen. Maybe he had to do it. I don't know, but we didn't like it. Anyhow, the tragedy was that Erna went to Paris and came back with a whole bunch of leaflets against the Nazis, was caught at the border with them, and came to the room, set the police after her. And I tell you, we burned those like nothing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you burn them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a stove, because they were wood stoves. And in Paris, I don't know, a few hours, not many hours later, the police was there, and not only interrogated us but took us and have-- we both were arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contacts with the ISK before she returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is-- and she is also ISK. And not only that, we had contact in Vienna with social democrats and some very fine people. And all-- if I wouldn't be so terribly about names. One of them was a very-- I thought she was a famous writer, Lily, first name, but I can't think of the last name. Jewish. And she wrote a lot of books. And we were very, very close to them. So there was contact. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were arrested and came into different places. I will do this very quick. The horrible thing was in the police station, because it was filthy dirty, your leg was on a slab. And it was the dirtiest place I've ever been in. And you weren't allowed to wash. And it's just awful. But then a young girl was brought. And I have to say that because I learned so many other things of life that I think we forget. Was just a nice little girl, and she was bought in because I think she had stolen a few pears or something. I forgot what. But there she was, this little girl who dreamt of having a flower shop. I still remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had somebody to talk to. And I always say with a half smile, over the weekend, it was a circus. There were all these fat women that were in the street corners in Vienna selling apples and pears and so on. But they were forbidden to move. But they always moved because they wouldn't sell enough in one place. So they were arrested always for the weekends. So these women would come in there, in the same cell where I was. And I was young and wanted to keep always elastic. So I knew how to do these kind of things. They did it with me. They thought it was very funny that we did that. And it's a kind of an almost a comical thing when I tell it. It wasn't so comical because I thought I would be sended back to the Nazis, you know? Also, I have to say some little thing. When we walked through the underground tunnel from the one place to this place, the advances that they do to young girls, amazing, you know? And I never quite understand it. But all my life, I seem to have been able to say, 'Oh, leave me alone.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But sometimes I think I was perhaps not pretty enough. I don't know. But, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was pretty stupid in that thing, but then they put me into what they called a concentration camp. [INAUDIBLE] in Vienna. And I think it was in the camp [? Mauthausen ?] but I cannot guarantee that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just maintained underground until they were ready to move you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's not really underground. It's in a police station. They wanted to take my glasses away, and I screamed. I said, I can't see. And I still remember one big policeman said, 'Let her go.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, anyhow, I get into that concentration camp. And this is another almost a joke, but it wasn't a joke at the time. But the head officer concentration camp says, 'What do I do? This is all men, one woman. What's she doing?' 'Well, we have no other place to put her.' So I heard this command, and all the doors that were open had to be closed. And I had to pass by. And they put me into one room, which was almost as large as this one. And was all by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: They still didn't allow me to read anything. And it was very odd. It was very different. I didn't know what they wanted. But the-- a physician came and asked me how I am and all this kind of strange thing. I will not go again into all this. They-- I have to say always the things that are good too, instead of the bad ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a man who was a prisoner. And he came in, and they made him put some wood on the stove. And he saw me, and he said, 'You have nothing to read?'

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not allowed to. And ten minutes later, a newspaper was pushed under the door for me. And everywhere on this margins was something like be courageous. It was incredible. Now, they asked me also by this kind of, are you Nazi or are you Sozi because they were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Sozi mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Social. Social democrats. So either Nazi or social democrats. And I said I won't answer because I thought it was much too dangerous. I didn't know in what environment I was. I will not answer. But I have to say, then we're not going into detail anymore on that, but it was strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything else that you saw there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- not cruelty, not like in the German part. Just all these people pushed together. A lot of them. And then to think that the Nazis and Sozis were together? How could they ever? I mean, it's just-- no. I did not see much more there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were isolated because you were a female?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was isolated. I was totally in that room. I-- I can't tell you what happened. I can't tell you anymore when they come and say you can go. But Erna was still kept. I was left go. Why? Nobody knows. I have no idea. All I can now tell is that Erna came out too. And I was still teaching some kids. So I had been away, and it wasn't so long. This wasn't a very long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But suddenly there was droning in the sky. Then they turned on the radio and there, Seyss-Inquart, you know, it wasn't-- what was-- was that his name? The one from-- the Nazi in-- in-- in-- in-- in Austria. S-E-I-S-S hyphen, and I think it's Inquart, I-N-Q-U-A-R-D. But I might spell it wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And saying loud, 'Austrians, the liberation is coming. The Germans are coming. They will not do any harm to us. It will be much better.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So anybody who ever tells me that Austria was invaded, it was not. They greeted it with open arms. But I know that evening that this was happening, that we heard that voice, and I went out to teach something, you know, to these people there. And when I came into the street, an old woman stood there and cried and cried. And I said, 'What is it?' And she picked up a leaflet, and there it was, that the Nazis were already in. They came, but I saw with tables, with chairs. They brought everything in so that they were settled already immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will not, again, go into all the details. We got Erna very quickly out to Paris because she had a passport. I had lost my passport. I was-- I was stuck. No nothing anymore, and I was sure I would die anyhow. But I have to tell you that Vienna was as bad after that as anything I've seen in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see? How did the Nazis come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I saw, with my own eyes, young, pregnant women-- and certainly they were Jewish-- pulled out of their rooms or street. I mean, they were pulled into the street and made to scrub the streets. The streets had been full of slogans. They were made to scrub the streets. One, two. Because I was sure that I would die anyhow, so I went anywhere. I went into the market. There were two SS men. In the middle was a young woman. And she had stamped on her brow, 'I slept with a Jew.' And they were going with her and were constantly hitting her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was constantly hitting her?

speaker: interviewee
text: These two brownshirts. And I can't tell you whether they were German or whether they were Austrian, but you can be sure the Austrians were all there just with them because they couldn't be that-- but then the Germans came in. The Germans were ready to take over. But they were very delighted. And so this, I have seen. I had to leave that place where I stayed first. That woman cried because she had a Jewish friend and said, 'This will be the end for everything.' I came into an apartment, strange enough, where-- and how I got there, I don't know. But there were two, perhaps around their 20s, sisters. And they were Jews, Separdic Jews. I don't know where they came, I think from Turkey originally, but I can't say that for sure. We were afraid of each other because we didn't know. I didn't know who they were. They didn't know who I was. We found out after a while that we were listening behind the doors what somebody else was saying until we found out. And then they told me that they had somebody who would get them out of the country very quick. And they shared with me some, I have never forgotten, potatoes because I had nothing to eat. Oh, I went to that place where I had tutored this one kid. The place was torn up. The Nazis had already come in and torn up things. But the kid wanted to go to school, and perhaps eight years. And the mother said, 'Be careful when you go to school. Don't say anything.' And he-- don't say anything when they spit on you. He said, 'I spit back.' And the mother says, 'If you spit back, your father will be killed.' I have always used that in my classes as an example how one can destroy courage in children. And it's understandable. You understand the mother. You know what's happening to that child. But that I saw with my own eyes, and I lived through it. I was with these people. I was sure that it would be the end anyhow. When a letter arrived from England, because that's where my sister was, and England wasn't as suspicious to be as from France. And we had a way of writing. We knew how to write a code. And she gave me exactly when I should leave by train to Innsbruck, and that in Innsbruck would be somebody who would wear white gloves. And I was supposed to wear white gloves. And she would help me that I get into France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an idea of code, the code that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, I don't know whether I'd even still know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is terrible. You will think I pretend, but I don't remember it very well. It had something to do with letters. That we knew certain words started, but I can't tell it you for sure. But we had worked that out before we got-- knew we-- that we would get into prisons. And this came partially from my sister. I knew it came also from Paul, you know. But I-- the train, oh, there was somebody else from the underground in Vienna who said, 'I will be there when you get on that train again. Watch that you get on that train,' which was, again, very touching and very courageous. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I got to Innsbruck, I get off the train. And there's that sweet, little, young woman with white gloves. And I will not go into all the details, but she took me to her hotel room. And it was torture for me because she told me that they had falsified a passport. It was, I think-- I forgot. Probably an Austrian. I don't know anymore. And there's my picture in it. But the awful thing was that I would have to go through Munich, stop and change trains in Munich, and then go on a train that would get me to Paris. I said, 'I can't go into Germany again. I just can't take it.' And I still remember she said, 'You know, I admire you. You are the heroes, not I. So you will do it.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to cry. And I said, 'No, you are. Why do you do that? You don't even know me.' And she said, 'No. And I will never know your name, and you will never know my name. And all I can tell you, I'm just a little minister's daughter in Switzerland.' So you see, everywhere, there was somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all. I will never know her name. And I got the stuff. I had to learn by heart. It was not my name. It was another name, I think. I forgot. Anyhow, I had to learn the heart of the story of that person, whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The girl-- the woman?

speaker: interviewer
text: The person's name on the passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe it was my name. But I-- no, it wasn't my name. It's impossible. No. I had to learn all that. But it was my picture, that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went from Austria--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into Germany by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- You should know that when I got on the train in Austria-- in-- in Innsbruck, a whole bunch of young Germans come in, and jubilant. This is now one country. We are-- it's just wonderful. And I was thinking, 'Oh, my God. If there's anybody who knows me from somewhere.' And, you know, then they said, 'You're so quiet.' And I said, 'Yeah. I'm not feeling well.' And-- oh, yes. Now I remember. I had to say that my mother-- my father was French. And I-- something. I had no accent. I didn't have an accent. And I said, 'Oh, my mother lived a long time in Germany.' Something like that. It's horrible. See, it's good for me because I forget the lies. I always hated lying. And I had to do it. But it was awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was jubilant. But then you get into Munich, and with my own eyes-- now this is the Anschluss. And this is-- I think we can look that up. It's 90, isn't it? Anschluss, A-N-S-C-H-L-U-S-S. That's when Austria becomes part of Germany. Now it's either '38 or '30-- no. It's '38. It's '38. It's '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were jubilant in Austria. They were jubilant in Munich. The drinking, you know. Beer and, oh, they were celebrating all over. And I had to sit there and wait for the next train. And then the train went. And just before the French-- yeah, no, at the French border, there was a Frenchman in my-- in the same place where I was sitting. And when we were just entering the border, they called him out. And I thought, 'Oh, my God. Now it's again the end.' And so you can see, I'm not always without apprehension or happy, anything like that. And I thought maybe he has heard. He always asked me why I was so quiet. And I always had to explain that I'm sick and not feel very well and stuff like that. But he came back, and that dumb idiot had smuggled some liquor into France. So this is what I was sitting there with. But we were-- I-- I always say when we were across the border, it was as if the train sounded different. I can't describe it. It was singing. It was a totally different sound. Now, I was not finished by any means. And I will try to make it as fast as I can. When I get into Paris, Paul is in terrible-- I mean, in a tiny little room full with bed bugs, hardly any work, hating being a refugee because you feel you are no more really active. He wants to go back and kill Hitler. That was his only idea. And he had done so many things that I didn't even go into. And I say it in my book that being an immigrant is not in that position. It's not something very nice. The people have become angry. They have let down. They don't have a real task. And they don't love each other very much anymore. I mean, there's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Paul knew where to meet you when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he did not-- no. He wasn't at the station by no means. But then we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we both cried. We just couldn't believe it. And we knew they would come into France, too. But we said, for a little bit, you know, for a little bit we are still there. But there was so much to tell also because when I had been the first time, taken by the Nazis, he had been wanting to come into Germany and do something. And the organization forbid him to do that. You know, you had to do what they said. And I was told that he come-- they didn't find him anymore. They couldn't find him anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the ISK still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But they couldn't find him. And finally somebody went to his room where he was. This is when I was in the Nazi camp place. And he was just lying there, just starving. He felt that when I was gone, that he wanted to go too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- well, in between, and I didn't go into that. Once we had seen each other before. I can't-- there's sometimes things I forget. Where had you seen each other before?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in the beginning in Vienna and had an Austrian passport, I was allowed to go to Paris. And that was pure heaven. This was a little interval, and I've never forgot we saw the van Gogh exhibit.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, art is just this wonderful thing, you know? But that, I forgot to mention because I can't think of everything. I have to mention-- can I show this book? No. You were still free to move then from Vienna to Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time. This is before--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Anschluss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the Anschluss and before I am arrested in Vienna. It's before the arrest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the second time now you saw Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this time it was in Paris, outside of Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris, very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you are a refugee. You have to get permission to stay. People stand for hours at the prefecture, P-R-E-F-E-C-T-U-R-E. They don't get the paper. You know, you may get a paper, and you may not get a paper. The French flic, F-L-I-C, that's the police, are make fun of all those dumb people. It's not very nice. People aren't always nice to refugees. We were a nuisance. We thought we were great allies, but they didn't see us that way. We were-- we were a nuisance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I couldn't get papers for staying in Paris. Paul had difficulties with that too. He was also, I forgot when that was, but I can't tell you when. The French once arrested him for four weeks just because he had no papers. I mean, it's that kind of thing. And so I had to leave Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: With him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was impossible. And we wouldn't get permanently to stay anywhere or anything like that. So we were separated all the time. And I got to Lyon, where we knew some people. And I could work as a maid. And it's the dirtiest treatment that I got from one of the women there.
